medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.commonUse: Relief of mild to moderate pain and fever
medications.typicalFrequency: Every 6-8 hours as needed, not exceeding 4 doses per day
medications.notes: Widely used and generally well tolerated at recommended doses; exceeding the daily limit can stress the liver.

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.commonUse: Bacterial infections of the ear, throat, sinuses, and respiratory tract
medications.typicalFrequency: Every 8 hours, for the full prescribed course
medications.notes: Should be taken for the complete duration prescribed even if symptoms improve early, to avoid antibiotic resistance.

medications.name: Metformin
medications.commonUse: Blood sugar management in type 2 diabetes
medications.typicalFrequency: Once or twice daily with meals
medications.notes: Usually taken with food to reduce stomach upset; routine monitoring of kidney function is often recommended.

medications.name: Atorvastatin
medications.commonUse: Lowering LDL cholesterol and reducing cardiovascular risk
medications.typicalFrequency: Once daily, typically in the evening
medications.notes: Grapefruit juice can interact with statins; periodic liver function checks are commonly advised.

medications.name: Amlodipine
medications.commonUse: Management of high blood pressure and certain types of chest pain
medications.notes: Mild ankle swelling is a recognised side effect; blood pressure should be monitored regularly.

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.commonUse: Reducing stomach acid for reflux, ulcers, and heartburn
medications.notes: Best taken on an empty stomach roughly 30 minutes before eating for optimal effect.

medications.name: Cetirizine
medications.commonUse: Relief of allergy symptoms such as sneezing, itching, and runny nose
medications.notes: May cause mild drowsiness in some individuals; non-drowsy for most adults at standard doses.

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.commonUse: Pain relief, inflammation, and fever reduction
medications.typicalFrequency: Every 6-8 hours with food, not exceeding label limits
medications.notes: Taking with food reduces stomach irritation; not typically recommended for prolonged unsupervised use.
